name: Jayarasi Bhatta
dates_notes: Jayarāśi is usually dated to the late eighth or early ninth century; the range follows the standard dating used for the Tattvopaplava-siṃha in modern studies.
core_thesis: Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa is the author of the *Tattvopaplava-siṃha*, the exceptional Cārvāka-adjacent Sanskrit treatise that survives as a continuous text rather than as hostile fragments. His school placement should be stated carefully: the text is often classified as Lokāyata or Cārvāka, but its method is radical skepticism rather than a simple statement of materialist doctrine. Jayarāśi examines the standard means of knowledge and argues that their definitions cannot establish stable *tattvas*. The work opens by turning the promised inquiry into reality against the very possibility of such reality-claims, and its first major section takes up perception by testing definitions such as the Nyāya formula for *pratyakṣa* ([*Tattvopaplava-siṃha* 1](cite://jayarasi-bhatta/tattvopaplavasimha/1)). Its importance for the roster is documentary: unlike the reconstructed Bārhaspatya fragments, this is a surviving authorial skeptical text.

engaged_works.title: Tattvopaplavasimha
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Jayarāśi's late-eighth- or early-ninth-century period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely attributed to Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa in the edited Sanskrit tradition; classification as Cārvāka, Lokāyata, or sui generis skepticism remains debated.

ascription_evidence.description: The Baroda edition and later studies transmit the text as Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa's Tattvopaplava-siṃha.
Modern scholarship debates whether the text should be counted as Cārvāka/Lokāyata or as an independent skeptical work.

engaged_works.summary: A skeptical Sanskrit treatise testing and destabilizing the accepted *pramāṇas* and the *tattvas* established through them. The text proceeds through analyses of perception, inference, comparison, testimony, and associated realist assumptions. It is Cārvāka-adjacent because it shares the anti-inferential and anti-śāstric pressure visible in Lokāyata reports, but it is methodologically more destructive than a straightforward positive materialist system. The GRETIL plaintext is now on disk.
